extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin (PT) is a thermal incubator consisting of an insulated styrofoam box that houses a heat‑bed for heating, a fan for air circulation, two internal temperature sensors measuring the air temperature inside the box, one external temperature sensor measuring ambient temperature, and a Raspberry Pi that runs the control software. The plant is a Cyber‑Physical System (CPS) whose purpose is to reach a target temperature inside the box and maintain it regardless of the contents, using the fan and heat‑bed to distribute heat uniformly. The setup represents a small‑scale, representative thermal regulation system suitable for digital‑twin experimentation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides real‑time visualization of incubator status via a time‑series database and dashboard; on‑demand model calibration using automated calibration algorithms; anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation through a Kalman filter; predictive what‑if simulations enabled by co‑simulation of historical or live PT data; optimisation of controller parameters; a closed‑loop self‑adaptation process that detects plant behaviour changes, schedules experiments, re‑calibrates models, re‑configures the Kalman filter and controller, and updates the PT accordingly; and continuous monitoring of temperature data and safety checks.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation uses a RabbitMQ message broker for synchronisation and communication between the Raspberry Pi controller, low‑level driver, and DT components; a low‑level driver abstracts sensor/actuator I/O and enforces a safe protocol; a bang‑bang style controller (with lower/upper temperature limits, heating and waiting durations) runs on the Raspberry Pi; calibration algorithms update model parameters; a Kalman filter performs state estimation; co‑simulation infrastructure runs what‑if scenarios; a time‑series database stores sensor data for dashboards; the entire hardware and software stack is open‑source and hosted on a GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin is organised around a message‑oriented middleware pattern using a central RabbitMQ server. The physical twin’s low‑level driver reads temperature data periodically and publishes it to RabbitMQ; the controller subscribes to these messages, receives commands from RabbitMQ, and publishes its control states. DT components (e.g., calibration, monitoring, simulation modules) also subscribe to the same message streams, enabling decoupled interaction between cyber and physical elements and supporting the calibration, anomaly detection, what‑if simulation, and self‑adaptation loops described in the case study.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is achieved through the RabbitMQ message broker. The low‑level driver, controller, and DT services communicate by publishing and subscribing to messages on RabbitMQ, allowing other applications or enterprise systems that can connect to the broker to exchange sensor data, control commands, and status information.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The incubator Digital Twin integrates two analytical behavioural models of the thermal dynamics (Model A and Model B). Both models are derived from the heat‑equation Q = c·m·ΔT and predict the temperature inside the insulated styro‑foam box. Model A assumes that all electrical power (V·I·Δt) is transferred to the air (assumption 4) and uses two free parameters; Model B relaxes this assumption, incorporates the heat‑bed inertia, and uses four free parameters, providing higher fidelity. Sensor data—two temperature measurements from inside the box and one from outside—are collected by the low‑level driver, transmitted via the RabbitMQ message broker, and persisted in a time‑series database. Calibration algorithms continuously ingest this streamed data to estimate and update the model parameters in real time, linking data and models. The DT also hosts algorithms such as a Kalman filter for state estimation, anomaly detection, and what‑if simulations that consume both the calibrated models and the live sensor data to support monitoring, optimisation and decision‑making.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical actuators consist of a heating element (heat‑bed) and a fan installed inside the styro‑foam incubator. Actuation commands are generated by a bang‑bang controller running on a Raspberry Pi, which uses configurable parameters: lower temperature limit (LL), upper temperature limit (UL), heating duration (H) and waiting duration (C). The controller sends on/off commands for the heat‑bed and fan to the low‑level driver via a RabbitMQ communication server, enabling remote control over the network. A safe‑state protocol is embedded in the low‑level driver to prevent unsafe actuation; the driver periodically checks for commands and enforces safety constraints before applying them.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Three temperature sensors constitute the sensing layer: two sensors monitor the air temperature inside the incubator box and one sensor measures the ambient temperature outside the box. The low‑level driver periodically reads these sensors and uploads the readings to the RabbitMQ server, where they are stored in a time‑series database. The documents do not specify sampling frequency, transmission data rate, or detailed accuracy/precision specifications; only that the data are collected over time for use in calibration and monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is addressed by developing two models with differing abstraction levels; experimental results (Fig. 13) show that Model B, which accounts for heat‑bed inertia, yields higher accuracy in predicting system magnitude and time scale, thus being the preferred model for the DT. Validation is performed through calibration experiments where sensor data are used to estimate model parameters, and through anomaly detection via a Kalman filter that compares measured temperatures with model‑predicted heat‑bed temperature, exposing discrepancies. Uncertainty sources stem from the modelling assumptions (uniform temperature distribution, negligible wall heat storage, constant specific heat capacity, and power‑to‑heat conversion). Calibration, on‑demand recalibration, and continuous monitoring constitute the quality‑assurance process, while the safe‑protocol in the low‑level driver mitigates risks from erroneous actuation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature data are obtained from three sensors inside the insulated styro‑foam box and one sensor outside the box. The low‑level driver periodically reads the temperature values from the PT, optionally averages the two interior sensors (T2 and T3) to obtain an average box temperature, and publishes the measurements as messages to a RabbitMQ communication server. The messages contain the temperature reading (in °C) together with a timestamp. Digital‑Twin components subscribe to the RabbitMQ broker and receive these sensor‑data messages in real time for visualization, calibration and anomaly‑detection. The transmission protocol is AMQP over TCP/IP; the exact sampling period is not stated, but the driver operates on a periodic basis.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The controller (running on a Raspberry Pi) receives control commands from the RabbitMQ server that specify actions for the fan and the heat‑bed (e.g., turn on, turn off, heating duration, waiting duration). The controller forwards these commands to the low‑level driver, which executes the actuator states on the physical twin. A safe‑protocol is incorporated in the low‑level driver to keep the device in a safe state before applying commands. The controller also publishes its own control‑state messages back to RabbitMQ, providing feedback that can be consumed by the digital twin. Message formats and authentication details are not described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The communication backbone of the incubator digital twin is a RabbitMQ message broker. Components (sensors, low‑level driver, controller, DT services) connect to the broker using the AMQP protocol over TCP/IP. The network topology follows a publish/subscribe pattern: PT sensors → low‑level driver → RabbitMQ → controller and DT subscribers. RabbitMQ is described as a mature, lightweight, and easy‑to‑deploy server that can run on‑premise or in the cloud, providing reliability through its built‑in message queuing and delivery guarantees. Specific bandwidth, latency or redundancy mechanisms are not quantified in the source material.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering workflow starts with the construction of two physics‑based models (Model A and Model B) derived from explicit thermal assumptions about the incubator. Sensor readings from two internal and one external temperature sensors are collected by a low‑level driver, packaged as messages and sent to a RabbitMQ communication server. Calibration algorithms consume these data streams to perform on‑demand parameter estimation, updating the selected model in real time. The calibrated model feeds monitoring components and a Kalman filter that provides heat‑bed state estimation and anomaly detection. The DT framework also supports co‑simulation (what‑if simulations) that combine historical or live PT data with the model to explore controller parameter optimisations. A self‑adaptation loop automatically detects behavioural changes, schedules data‑gathering experiments, re‑calibrates the model, re‑configures the Kalman filter and updates the bang‑bang controller (parameters LL, UL, H, C). All components communicate via RabbitMQ, but no explicit version‑control, testing or quality‑assurance practices are described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The DT provides decision support through real‑time visualisation on a dashboard fed by a time‑series database, anomaly detection and heat‑bed temperature estimation using a Kalman filter built on Model B, and what‑if simulations via co‑simulation that evaluate alternative controller settings. The self‑adaptation loop uses detected anomalies to trigger experiments, recalibrate models and automatically re‑configure the controller, thereby generating actionable recommendations for maintaining target temperature despite disturbances.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The low‑level driver incorporates a safe protocol that keeps the physical twin in a safe state; communication between the controller, low‑level driver and other components is performed through a RabbitMQ message broker, which synchronises messages but the documents do not detail authentication, encryption, or access‑control mechanisms. Safety functions are supported by a Kalman‑filter based anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation that run on the digital twin to monitor deviations from expected behaviour. No explicit cybersecurity protocols, threat‑mitigation strategies, or formal risk‑assessment procedures are described in the provided documents.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware, software, and datasets are available online [6] . a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. **2.2.1** **The Communication Server**


For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular
open source message brokers. RabbitMQ is a mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy
on premises and in the cloud.

The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control
states to the RabbitMQ server.
We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed
between the controller and the load level driver, as illustrated in fig. 7.


**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the
